Just make sure you properly reset any global variables between requests.

> standardized API for programming sockets with async?

There are still some tricky bits here, like how curio and trio disagree about which operations should be marked async (though this is more of an issue for stuff like locks and events than sockets), and how asyncio's lowest portable abstraction layer is higher-level than sockets, and how maybe you want to use trio's higher-level layer instead of raw sockets. But generally yeah, the way I think about it is that these libraries are basically some concurrency system + some cancellation system + a bunch of I/O primitives, and most of the innovation is in the first two; the I/O primitives are fairly obvious and boring in comparison.

That's the nice thing about "mostly synchronous" libraries like urllib3: they only need the I/O layer :-). urllib3 in particular is a *little* more complicated, because it turns out that high-quality HTTP clients need a *little* bit of internal concurrency (specifically sending and receiving simultaneously on a single socket), but we have an actual working cross-library socket abstraction layer that exposes what urllib3 needs:

https://github.com/python-trio/urllib3/tree/bleach-spike/urllib3/_backends

(or eventually this will probably move to https://github.com/python-trio/urllib3/tree/bleach-spike/src/urllib3/_backends , depending on when you read this comment)
Curio requires Python 3.5+. You are running Python 3.4.3.

@jkbbwr appears to be assuming that everyone here is familiar with how Erlang handles task death signaling, which might be a bit optimistic :-). Here's an excellent article on how [Erlang handles these kinds of things in general](http://ferd.ca/the-zen-of-erlang.html). IIUC, the way linking in particular works is:

* Links are bidirectional, and by default the lifetimes of the tasks are "linked" in the sense that if one dies, they both die. (And this is of course transitive, so if you think of links as making edges in a graph, then when one task dies, its whole connected component dies.) This kind of bidirectional linked-fates semantics seems handy for lots of common simple cases like, for example, the worker tasks in `curio.subprocess.Popen.communicate`, where you want parent cancellation to trigger child cancellation, and child crashes to propagate and cancel the other children.

* A given task can override this behavior, so that if one of its linked tasks dies, then instead of getting canceled it instead receives a message in its "mailbox". (All Erlang processes have a dedicated "mailbox", which is an incoming mostly-FIFO message queue.)

(Erlang also has "monitors", which are a way for a task to request to be notified when some arbitrary other task dies. This is different from links in that monitors are unidirectional, never kill the receiving process, and there can be multiple monitors watching the same task.)

So the obvious problem here is that Curio does not have the concept of signals, or task mailboxes :-). I've actually wondered independently whether we should generalize the current cancellation concept to something more like a "cancel signal" that has the default behavior of raising an exception, but where processes have the ability to redirect that to some sort of message queue. (Similar to how SIGTERM by default kills a Unix process, but the process can either mask it temporarily -- like `curio.defer_cancellation` -- or register an arbitrary handler -- curio doesn't have an analogy for this.) If we did have a more general "signal system" like this then Erlang-style links might make more sense?

Agreed with @dabeaz though that something more like Erlang's "monitors" with an explicit queue are definitely the thing to add first.

I don't really have a sense for what algorithms might work here. So far, my experience has always been that it's better to spend time manually labeling data than to grind on a semi-supervised algorithm. I should qualify that by saying that I do find it to be very useful to train a model on a small dataset, then to use that model to label my unlabled data. Then I retrain the model on the whole dataset and look at the training instances where the new model disagrees with the labels.  Then I adjust the labels and repeat the train and then manual review process a few times.

For instance, if you are making a dog/not-dog classifier you can take all the images that are now labeled as dog and sort them based on the model output so that things predicted to be not-dog come first.  Generally there will be a lot of obvious labeling mistakes there and the sorting makes it really easy to flip their labels.

This "relabel, retrain, predict, find outliers, repeat" loop is something I've done a lot and it's very hard to beat.  For instance, the face recognition model I posted is trained on about 3 million faces. I personally labeled most of the images in this dataset using the above procedure. I forget how much time I ended up spending on the data curation, maybe 40 hours? That's not that much time in the grand scheme of things. Face recognition is relatively tedious because there are so many identities.  But for binary classification it's super easy to label datasets of hundreds of thousands of images this way and end up with a dataset containing very high quality labels. Like if you were at work and you had to really make it work, spending a week grinding on dataset improvement is generally a much better use of time than spending months tweaking algorithms.  Most people vastly underestimate the importance of data curation.

Anyway, that isn't to say semi-supervised learning isn't important.  At the end of the day I hate labeling data. And obviously humans are able to do amazing semi-supervised learning. So we know it's possible and an algorithm that can really do it would be amazing and super useful.  But the bar is quite high since the baseline procedure I suggested above is very effective.  I've never seen an algorithm that can complete with it.  Although that link you sent is saying there have been some great improvements this year. I haven't read those papers yet so maybe this is the year we finally have good semi-supervised algorithms :)

I'm also totally cool with adding reference implementations of published semi-supervised algorithms even if they aren't ultimately superior to the manual review process. So if anyone wants to submit a PR with one that's awesome.

Also, that note in this issue template is not directed at you or questions like this.  It's for people who obviously don't know how to program and are trying to use the issue tracker as a free code writing service. I get a lot of questions that can basically be rephrased as "I have no idea how to program, can you tell me what keys to press to make a program that does X? Where X is some trivial modification to one of the example programs."  Like "how do I save the detected faces to a file?" or some such question.

I actually kept a similar idea boiling in my head for quite a while now. The idea was to cleanly separate things that Jedi can confidently reason about and the rest. For example, jedi can trace the path of a function argument within a function, but it cannot with certainty say where did the argument come from in the first place. That would be an "entrypoint".

The simplest way to think about "entrypoints" was to take a module — since Jedi as of now operates on per-module basis — and try to find all the places that could change about that module, that would be, from the top of my head:
- the name of the module in question, which depends on import path
- imported modules and objects from those modules, which depend on import path and hooks
- arguments of publicly accessible functions (local functions should be OK)
- values of module-accessible variables (external code might inject variables or change existing ones)

Once we identify an "entrypoint", there should be a way for a plugin to help Jedi core to resolve that entrypoint using heuristics or other a priori knowledge. For example, docstring parser that tries to guess types of the variables would become a plugin. There also could be a plugin that would scan a certain directory and subdirectories looking for usages of a function and returning found argument types. Or a plugin that traces the execution of a certain script and returns arguments found that way.

In that scenario, a metaclass might simply become another entrypoint to be handled by plugins.

> surely you wouldn't rely on package authors to publish wheels then, but have something of your own where you create wheels for projects that don't have them. Otherwise spending so much cycles doesn't sound like a good idea.

If package authors publish wheels, there's no need for package users to create their own special wheels for their CIs, it reduces duplicated effort for those who want them, and end users also benefit.

> Or is every project out there except pycparser already publishing wheels?

There's a big benefit in wheels for Python projects needing to compile C, especially for Windows where setting up a dev environment with a compiler can be a real problem. But for pure Python, source-only isn't generally such a big problem.

Heres's some benefits, and stats on the most-downloaded packages on PyPI (which includes pycparser): 226/360 use wheels. http://pythonwheels.com/

---

On our CI, pycparser is installed as part of `pip install cffi`. Different Python versions had different combinations of wheel and source available. Here's how many seconds the combined download and installation operation took.

This is my preliminary version for markdown, along with a test at the end. Note that I split the string into a list of chars before escaping, so as not to affect the offsets in entities. Another subtlety is that special characters contained within an entity should not be escaped (this may be different in HTML).

```python
def format_markdown(string, entities):
    # incomplete 
    char_map = {'*': '\\*',
                '_': '\\_',}

    formatter = {'bold':      lambda s,e: '*'+s+'*',
                 'italic':    lambda s,e: '_'+s+'_',
                 'text_link': lambda s,e: '['+s+']('+e['url']+')',
                 'code':      lambda s,e: '`'+s+'`',
                 'pre':       lambda s,e: '```text'+s+'```'}

    def inside_some_entities(i):
        return any(map(lambda e: e['offset'] <= i < e['offset']+e['length'], entities))

    # Escape special characters
    seq = map(lambda c,i:
                  char_map[c]
                      if c in char_map and
                          not inside_some_entities(i) else c,
              list(string),          # split string to char sequence
              range(0,len(string)))  # along with each char's index

    offset = 0
    result = ''

    for e in entities:
        f,n,t = e['offset'], e['length'], e['type']

        result += ''.join(seq[offset:f])

        if t in formatter:
            result += formatter[t](''.join(seq[f:f+n]), e)
        else:
            result += ''.join(seq[f:f+n])

        offset = f + n

    result += ''.join(seq[offset:])
    return result


entities = [
    {'type': 'bold', 'offset': 0, 'length': 3},
    {'type': 'italic', 'offset': 3, 'length': 3},
    {'type': 'text_link', 'offset': 9, 'length': 6, 'url': 'www.yahoo.com'},
]

print format_markdown('a_bcdefh*ijklm*nopq', entities)
```

Dear William,

Thanks for your suggestions, and you were correct: the problem was indeed in the `ContentInfo` field with wrong name ('contentType' instead of 'content_type').

Some improvements would make implementors' life easier:
* In core.py:424, the debug function uses `%s`. When debugging CMS output, it often raises an exception when trying to print binary values contained in `OctetString`s (such as in `EncryptedContentInfo`, field `encrypted_content`). I suggest you to use `%r` instead.
* The Choice implementation does not permit a "pythonistic" construction (`dict`/`list`). As fair as I have understood, it requires always explicit class constructor with `name` and `value` parameters.
* When the user passes a name which does not name any of the specified fields, the library should raise an exception. Reason: I suppose the problem with wrong names (typos) will be usual when converting code from pyasn1, or when reading directly from ASN.1 specifications.

`get_channel` returns None when a channel with the specified channel ID was not found in the cache.
This raises some possibilities:
- The channel is not in cache yet. Try using `wait_until_ready`, which sleeps the coroutine until all channels, guilds and members are available in cache.
- This channel doesn't exist (you copied the ID wrong or similar)
- The bot logged in can't actually see the channel (this doesn't mean that it can't read from it and should only happen when not in a mutual guild)

Judging by your last comment, I'm going to guess it's the first one. Stick a ```py
await client.wait_until_ready()
``` before your main code and see if it fixes the problem.

I haven't been able to duplicate this directly, but one possible failure path for AutoCommit is this: we send a request to the broker. There is a network failure and the request fails. We do not get a socket event via selector (select / poll / epoll / kqueue / etc), and the request hangs indefinitely. No new autocommit request will be sent while an existing request is in-flight, so you might see exactly this behavior.

This failure scenario is addressed generally in #887 -- what do you think?

Hi Steve,

Sorry it's taken me so long to get back to you -- I've been ridiculously
busy lately, and the project I was going to use this for has been on the
back burner.

I suggested `BaseShape.is_group` because I like to try to avoid importing
classes for the sole purpose of type checking. Also, it feels similar
to `is_placeholder`,
which was also not in the MS API. Of course you can also do an explicit
type check, and for most other shape types I would suggest that approach,
but as group shapes are fundamentally different from other shapes (much
like placeholders), I think making an exception in this case is warranted.

Regarding `Group.shapes`: to my mind, `GroupItems` was a poor name choice.
You probably wouldn't be calling that on an arbitrary object (because it
throws an error for non-group shapes), which means you already know you're
dealing with a group, and `group_shape.group_items` looks kinda silly.
Plus, the "group" part could be misconstrued as a verb -- as though you are
trying to put items into a group. Granted, that is a very minor
consideration. In any case, `shapes` seemed to make sense, because once you
know you have a group, you probably want it to function similarly to a
`ShapeTree` -- and this way you don't need to remember which property to
access on which object type, if you want to find out what shapes are inside
your object, "shapes" just seems like the common-sense attribute name.

As a side note, I have been working with the MS Office DOM for over 10
years, and I would caution against trying to stick to it too closely. It is
far from perfect. Also, it was designed to function in a very different
ecosystem than your Python library. The lack of subclassing and inheritance
in VBA was likely a driving force behind many of the design decisions, and
you are free of those limitations.

Anyway, my use case is that I want to be able to update the data in a group
of charts in an existing template, which I do not want to have to modify
(i.e. ungroup the charts).

I don't really have the brain for proposals or test-writing at the moment,
and honestly for now I'm probably just going to roll with my fork so I can
get this project done, but I would like to work with you to get group
handling incorporated into master at some point, because I think it well
strengthen the library. By the way, python-pptx is a wonderful thing, and I
am quite stoked about the prospect of porting my myriad VBA projects over
to Python, aided by your library. :)

~
Dan

> __ASCWriter__

I put some prints in the code above and am finding that when BT loses data source, it does not see this code. Actually appears that BT is unaware that it is not getting data.

Let me first try to summarize my understanding:

  - Intraday bars (either minutes or seconds)
  - The volume of the current bar is compared to the volume of the previous bar with the same "time" (obviously ... not the same date)

  - Pre-market data (and maybe post) exists and has varying length (hence your comment about the alignment of the bars)

Be it so, my first idea would be

  - A Data Source which accepts another data source and filters any pre/post market data, delivering to the strategy/indicators only the "market" data

The alternative is to keep a mark of when the 1st bar of the previous day "market" data took place to understand how far away (distance) in the past the to be compared bar is.

The second idea is implemented below. There is a convenience "time2num" function to avoid comparing objects and rather compare float values.

Because the conditions of the "pre"market data is unknown to me I have added a "session_prestart" time which allows the period to be extended. Instead of a "time" it could be a factor to increase the session bars by.

As you see below, the "data" carries the timeframe and compression informatino and can be accessed by the indicator.

````
def time2num(tm):
    """
    Convert :mod:`time` to the to the preserving hours, minutes, seconds
    and microseconds.  Return value is a :func:`float`.
    """
    HOURS_PER_DAY = 24.0
    MINUTES_PER_HOUR = 60.0
    SECONDS_PER_MINUTE = 60.0
    MUSECONDS_PER_SECOND = 1e6
    MINUTES_PER_DAY = MINUTES_PER_HOUR * HOURS_PER_DAY
    SECONDS_PER_DAY = SECONDS_PER_MINUTE * MINUTES_PER_DAY
    MUSECONDS_PER_DAY = MUSECONDS_PER_SECOND * SECONDS_PER_DAY

    tm_num = (tm.hour / HOURS_PER_DAY +
              tm.minute / MINUTES_PER_DAY +
              tm.second / SECONDS_PER_DAY +
              tm.microsecond / MUSECONDS_PER_DAY)

    return tm_num


class RelativeBarVolume(bt.Indicator):
    lines = ('rbv',)

    params = (
        ('session_prestart', datetime.time(6, 30)),
        ('session_start', datetime.time(9, 30)),
        ('session_end', datetime.time(16, 00)),
    )

    def __init__(self):
        self._setperiod()

        # Convert to numbers to speed up comparisons
        self.session_start = time2num(self.p.session_start)
        self.session_end = time2num(self.p.session_end)

        # Initialize the mark
        self._mark = 0

    def _setperiod(self):
        # Period calculation
        minend = self.p.session_end.hour * 60 + self.p.session_end.minute
        # minstart = session_start.hour * 60 + session_start.minute
        # use prestart to account for market_data
        minstart = self.p.session_prestart.hour * 60 + \
            self.p.session_prestart.minute

        minutes = minend - minstart

        tframe = self.data._timeframe
        tcomp = self.data._compression

        if tframe == bt.TimeFrame.Seconds:
            minperiod = (minutes * 60)

        minperiod = (minperiod // tcomp) + tcomp

        # Inform the platform about the minimum period needs
        self.addminperiod(minperiod)

    def _setmark(self):
        # Get the time part from datetime
        tm, _ = math.modf(self.data.datetime[0])

        if tm == self.session.start:
            # Record the start of the session
            self._dist = self._mark - len(self)
            self._mark = len(self)

    def prenext(self):
        self._setmark()

    def next(self):
        self._setmark()

        if self.session_start <= tm <= self.session_end:
            # Calculate relative bar volume only within session limits
            pvol = self.data.volume[self._dist]
            self.lines.rbv[0] = self.data.volume[0] / pvol
````

If for any reason you need to make a ajax request then you need to avoid jQuery's ajax method when dealing with a binary response. javascript can't handle binary string well and jQuery response is always a json or a string - it's incapable of handling binary as the browser tries to parse the response
- use either xhr and set responseType to blob
- or use the new fetch api
```javascript
fetch(url, {method: 'post', body: data})
.then(res => res.blob())
.then(blob => saveAs(blob, "filename.ppt"))
```

The guiding principle behind the design of xmltodict is to implement **one simple mapping** between XML and nested dict+list+string structures resembling what you would get from a JSON document, no more, no less. I have no intention of making xmltodict a full fledged XML processing framework and I think that features like the one proposed in this ticket come at the expense of the library's ease of use and code simplicity/maintainability.

IMHO, if you're hitting xmltodict's limitations, you should use XPath over the excellent [lxml](http://lxml.de/).

Of course I could be wrong, if so please prove it with a pull request that I'll be glad to review and merge as long as the library remains easy to use, simple and maintainable.

Hi, the main reason is because Swagger (Open API) prefers explicit over implicit. There is no such primitive ('integer' without format) defined in spec.

You can still support this kind of definition by adding primitives to pyswagger, refer to [here](https://github.com/mission-liao/pyswagger/blob/master/docs/md/tutorial/extend_prim.md) for more details.

Here is a pseudo code that might work for the primitive you described:
```python
from pyswagger import SwaggerApp
from pyswagger.primitives import SwaggerPrimitive
from pyswagger.primitives._int import validate_int, create_int

# create a cusomized primitive factory
factory = SwaggerPrimitive()
# type == 'integer', format == ''
# type == 'integer', format == None
factory.register('integer', '', create_int, validate_int)
factory.register('integer', None, create_int, validate_int)

# init SwaggerApp with customized primitive factory
app = SwaggerApp.load(your_url, prim=factory)
```

There are a number of ways sorting can be ensured; the ones that came to my mind involve storing some time-related value in `Stream` instances, in `__init__`, like:

- `time.time()` (wall clock time since Epoch)
  - pros: simple, no changes in the API
  - cons: issues will occur if system time is adjusted towards the past during computation; depends on `time`
- `time.clock()` (process CPU time)
  - pros: simple, no changes in the API
  - cons: depends on `time`; issues with `multiprocessing`
- Instance counter in Stream **class** (each instance stores the counter value at the moment of construction, then increases it) - best IMO
  - pros: simple
  - cons: thread safety? I'm pretty sure class attributes access is atomic, though
- Each Node instance increases a counter whenever `.stream()` is called, and passes the number to Stream constructor.
  - pros: idk lol
  - cons: Node is not immutable any more, API changes

After one of these values is available, `outgoing_edge_map` can be an `OrderedDict` that is sorted according to the values.

This is a very interesting change, I like the idea.

I have some questions/comments about functionality tho:

* Shouldn't the thread be a daemon thread (eg: so interpreter exit is not blocked by it)?
* The `thread.join` in `_stop_lock_refresher` may block up to the interval the thread uses. I think it's undesirable to have `release`/`__exit__` to wait such high amount of time.
* The `_stop_lock_refresher` method doesn't check if the thread is alive. This can easily lead to a deadlock if thread is dead (eg, you use `fork` - the new process will not have any threads).

The mongodb client also use threads for some management stuff, would be useful to check how they handle thread cleanup/termination. See https://github.com/mongodb/mongo-python-driver/blob/master/pymongo/periodic_executor.py

        extrinsic = np.empty(n)
        for j in range(n):
            Mj = Nodes[j]

            extrinsic[j] = sum(np.array(Lr[Mj])[:,j])

        L_posteriori = extrinsic + Lc
        #x = np.array(L_posteriori <= 0).astype(int)
        x = np.array(extrinsic <= 0).astype(int)
        product = InCode(H,x)
        #print (count, product)

        if product or count >= max_iter:
            break
    # print (count)
    return np.array(L_posteriori <= 0).astype(int), Lq - Lc, extrinsic, product
```

Just to keep a record of what I have, this is the robust variance calculator I wrote for my purpose. It still has to be generalized for any group variable (it works for IDs only currently). Essentially, it needs another line to calculate the average within-groups (the actual value for the individual obs) then continue with the rest

I will try to work on it this weekend (I will also get to finishing the Aalen-Johansen estimator #413 and write up some simulation data to show why we care to use it in the presence of competing events)

```
def robust_var(km, survival, event_table, weights, C):
    unique_event_times = event_table['observed'].loc[event_table['observed'] >= 1].index
    zck_St_j = 0
    Stj_lag = 1
    var_j = [0]
    for j in unique_event_times:
        delta_ck = np.where(km.durations == j, 1, 0) * np.array(km.event_observed) * np.array(weights)
        gamma_ck = np.where(km.durations >= j, 1, 0) * np.array(weights)
        ri = float(event_table.loc[event_table.index == j]['n'].values)
        di = float(event_table.loc[event_table.index == j]['observed'].values)
        qi = di / ri
        Stj = float(survival.loc[survival.index == j].values)

        zck_qi = (delta_ck - qi * gamma_ck) / ri
        zck_St_j = (1 - qi) * zck_St_j + Stj_lag * zck_qi
        z_bar = np.sum(zck_St_j) / C
        var_Stj = C * np.sum((zck_St_j - z_bar) ** 2 / (C - 1))
        var_j.append(var_Stj)
        Stj_lag = Stj
    return var_j

```

For future visitors, I think this experiment proves that `.parent` is an implementation detail, and overriding it is a fundamentally broken thing to do:

```pycon
>>> import logging, logging_tree
>>> a = logging.getLogger('a')
>>> b = logging.getLogger('b')
>>> bcd = logging.getLogger('b.c.d')
>>> bcd.parent = a
>>> logging_tree.printout()
<--""
   Level WARNING
   |
   o<--"a"
   |   Level NOTSET so inherits level WARNING
   |
   o<--"b"
       Level NOTSET so inherits level WARNING
       |
       o<--[b.c]
           |
           o !-"b.c.d"
               Broken .parent! Messages propagate to "a"
               Level NOTSET so inherits level WARNING
>>> bc = logging.getLogger('b.c')
>>> logging_tree.printout()
<--""
   Level WARNING
   |
   o<--"a"
   |   Level NOTSET so inherits level WARNING
   |
   o<--"b"
       Level NOTSET so inherits level WARNING
       |
       o<--"b.c"
           Level NOTSET so inherits level WARNING
           |
           o<--"b.c.d"
               Level NOTSET so inherits level WARNING
```

If you override `.parent`, it may be invisibly rewritten if the _real_ parent (which hadn't been created yet) were created later, undoing your overriding of `.parent`. The `logging` module assumes that no-body else will be messing with that.

Hey @stevefox - thanks for raising the issue.

Yes, I started fiddling with the 2.0 spec and experimenting with ways to move this project forward. I am looking for help for both maintaining the existing version, going through existing PRs and issues as well as working on a new version which I foresee as a breaking change.

As for the roadmap, here is what I was thinking of:

- Python 3.4+, Django 1.9 + (maybe 1.8LTS?) and DRF 3.3+
- Use the Swagger 2.0 spec; expose a single spec file rather than calling separate HTTP requests. Performance is a problem when we have dozens of simultaneous requests.
- Use Python objects / dictionaries to override / customize Swagger properties for serializers, views and viewsets rather than parsing YAML in the docstring. Thinking of using some sort of class on the serializers, or views which would allow developers to modify documentation
- Use DRF serializers to validate and render the Swagger spec
- Support multiple "instances" of swagger with isolated configurations. This could be useful for supporting multiple APIs in a single Django codebase. This could be private vs. internal API, different versions, etc.
- Add introspection on a view's parsers and renderers to present appropriate parameters - i.e. only use form fields if the endpoint accepts form data, otherwise use body, etc.

The original driving force behind this project (and its predecessor django-rest-framework-docs) stemmed from refusing to write documentation for an API I was building for an agency client. The idea of opening Word (gasps) each time code was changed was revolting to me (in 2012...) and wanted something to automate this process.  

Moving forward, I'd like to keep introspection and auto-generated docs at the core of the project. If we have to manually document the API in the codebase, we are following the same broken paradigm of updating a Word doc each time code changes. Better off writing a swagger spec in raw JSON/YAML and having 100%. However, I do want to make customization easier,  intuitive and more "pythonic" / "django-esque".

Would love to hear your thoughts and suggestions!

@tchellomello requested a similar feature in PR #46 [1]. But the way i remember it, i don't think there's a simple way to accomplish this. The way ring app worked was this:
1) Send a request to the ring servers to change the chime tone to a new one.
2) Poll for status updates until the message is sent to the ring device (sometimes this took over 10 seconds for my device).
3) Request the device to play the "current" chime tone (as it was changed in step 1 and confirmed in step 2).

So this could mean waiting 10 seconds before playing a custom chime. It would be unusable for realtime events (like garage door was opened etc) since it would take a while to play.

Also, this means changing the chime tone of the device for good. So we will have to spend another 10 seconds reverting it back to what it was before so that the usual doorbell chime isn't changed.

[1] https://github.com/tchellomello/python-ring-doorbell/pull/46#discussion_r139874432

To be a bit more explicit: if the function `f` has an input shape `inshape = (in1, in2, ...)` and output shape `outshape = (out1, out2, ...)` then `jacobian(f)(x)` has shape `outshape + inshape = (out1, out2, ..., in1, in2, ...)` and `hessian(f)(x)` has shape `outshape + inshape + inshape = (out1, out2, ..., in1, in2, ..., in1, in2, ...)`, which is consistent with just applying `jacobian` twice (indeed, that's how `hessian` is implemented). In the case of your example, we have `inshape = (4,)` and `outshape = (4,)`, so `jacobian(f)(x)` has shape `(4, 4)` and `hessian(f)(x)` has shape `(4, 4, 4)`. In the case of a scalar-valued function on vectors, we might have `inshape = (4,)` and `outshape = ()`, so that `hessian(f)(x)` has shape `(4, 4)`, which is a matrix as expected.

So I did some researching on the waiter implementation for waiting until a s3 object is restored, and I came to the conclusion that adding a waiter implementation is not the best approach. It would requires pulling of state for roughly 3 to 5 hours to see if a restoration is complete. In addition, for each pull of state a ``HeadObject`` request would be needed and in-between each call would require practically a sleeping for tens of minutes. This makes the implementation pretty inefficient in terms of time and cost.

The better implementation would be to expose a SNS notfication event for glacier restorations. There is already a concept in s3 via bucket notifications: http://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonS3/latest/dev/NotificationHowTo.html. We will talk with S3 to see if they could expose such a feature.

Otherwise, I think my PR addresses the other points that I listed.

The reason is for efficiency consideration to store offsets for `ParameterCollections/Parameter/LookupParameter`. It is a two-level offset hashmap for seeking.
In detail, say `ParameterCollection` `pc` contains millions of `Parameter`s. If all offsets are flat structured when loading we need to search in a large space. Maybe there is better design but currently, I use this for simplicity. Do you also feel it is tricky to understand? @neubig 
Another consideration is to avoid name confliction in `ParameterCollection`'s  `Parameter` with single `Parameter`.

As I understand it, this is Prasanth's option 2 + operator-name-indirection.

The operator-name-indirection is an interesting proposal. I am not sure if I fully understand if the resulting behavior is desirable or not (or immaterial) in the examples you discuss (such as Slice). 

For a tool (importer or exporter) that consciously checks the op. version and op. name (using the appropriate indirection, if we have it), the indirection presumably makes no difference.

We have an interesting effect if an exporter exports an operator-name (say "Slice") and this code is not made robust to versioning changes: that is, if it exports a newer op-version without changing the name "Slice", then the name "Slice" acquires a different (newer) semantics. 

I guess you are suggesting that this is a desirable effect? Perhaps, but I am not completely sure. This could also backfire in some instances.

@linkerzhang ideally I believe that's the case. If you read through the discussion mentioned in the PR msg, you'll hopefully agree that per-op attribute is not the best idea. One major reason (among others) is that it's not just about feature maps, filters are in different formats too.

Another motivation for doing so I'd like to re-emphasize is that it **localizes** the assumption made about tensor dimension order. For now, if a sequence of ops is going to be executed in NHWC order, we have something like:

```
transpose -> op_1 -> transpose^{-1} -> transpose -> op_2 -> transpose^{-1}...
```
Each op assumes that previous ops have handed them the tensor of the right order; this assumption is pretty wild and if one of the op fails to live up to its promise, we have a chain of disaster that is difficult to debug. 

If we have dimension denotation, the only assumption we are making is that denotation is correct, which is easy to check since denotation is contained in the proto object representing a tensor; and in each op, we simply need to assert that the tensor we received was in NCHW format.

Thanks for the clarification. I assume you meant

* `pipenv install --test` this will install [packages] and [test-packages]
* `pipenv install --dev` this will install [packages] and [dev-packages]

would this be correct? (Option names are up for discussion. The point is that you need `--dev` to explicitly install [dev-packages]; the default is to only install [packages].)

The question this brews would then be—Should it be possible to allow users to install all three sections? If no—what answer should we provide when users (inevitably!) come requesting this feature?  And if yes…

How are we going to separate packages in the lockfile so it can cover all possible use cases:

* Only [packages]
* [packages] + [dev-packages]
* [packages] + [test-packages]
* [packages] + [dev-packages] + [test-packages] (also, how do you order the latter two?)

Should this be generalised to include *arbitrary* numbers of sections? I can easily imagine people come asking for [doc-packages], [staging-packages] and so on. Warehouse, the project for the currently active pypi.org site, has [eight requirements.txt variants](https://github.com/pypa/warehouse/tree/master/requirements).

Syntactic-wise, this *could* be better:

```toml
[packages]
# ...

[dev-packages]
# ...

[extra-packages.test]    # This "extra-packages" prefix signifies a custom section.
# ...

[extra-packages.doc]
# ...
```

> Note: Dots in a TOML section name signifies a subkey, so in Python this would be parsed into
>
> ```python
> {
>     'packages': {...},
>     'dev-packages': {...},
>     'extra-packages': {
>         'test': {...},
>         'doc': {...},
>     },
> }
> ```
> 
> This way you can have arbitrary keys under extra-packages, and we can simply look it up.

But then we’ll go back to the previous section combination question, but instead of 4 you now have `n! - n + 1` combinations to consider.

> I see that pip-tools has brought in some local repository hashing
upstream, is this going to be impacted by that at all if we bring it over?

Good point. Aside from making the diff more complicated, I think it’s fine.

It’s slightly obscured, but the hash key is based on the full URL including
the fragment (and is only cached if the fragment provided some kind of
checksum) so it’s effectively like caching on a web ETag. Ie the server
would have to be lying to us about the hash, but we are already trusting
the server to give us the right stuff.

**You can correctly make the above query by doing the following:**

`az vm list --query "[?licenseType == 'Windows_Server']" -o table`

**For an explanation of why this is the correct way of writing the filter expression query, please see below.**

There are two different ways to specify [string literals in JMESPath](http://jmespath.org/specification.html#raw-string-literals):

-     `"Windows_Server"`
Back ticks are used in literal expressions. So the above is a string literal expression.
-     'Windows_Server'
Using single quotes is a shorthand for writing a string literal expression.

The following is an alternative way of making the query you want.

    az vm list --query "[?licenseType==\`\"Windows_Server\"\`]" -o table

Notice that the back-ticks and double quotes are escaped. `echo  my_query` can be helpful for seeing what is actually passed as the query to the command.

Refs #1216 and #1215. I think we're trying too hard to be clever with an ad-hoc string query syntax and IMO we should leave the current syntax in place ("simple" syntax, where anything that can be converted to a number is a number and everything else is a string) and add a robust syntax that can encode any value as well as its type. I'd propose JSON (so `/records?min_target.version=53` for number, `/records?min_target.version="53"` for string, `/records?min_target.version=null` for null, etc.).

For comparison order, one reason I don't want to be too clever here is that I'm afraid of inventing an inconsistent order. E.g. 2.0 < 10 but 10 < "2.0b". Having a robust syntax doesn't completely avoid this problem because data in different records can be of different types, and we will have to order them somehow (if we get e.g. `_sort=different_typed_field`). How do we handle that? I don't think throwing an exception in this case is necessarily a bad thing, but if we have to invent an order, I think "all strings are less than all ints" is fine.

`add_source_file` is not done (I assume you mean called) in `ui.py` it is called from the Vivado example and it is there you need to make the modification. You need to modify the Vivado example such that it does not call `add_source_file` with .mif files. You to that by filtering the list of files using Python programming to remove the elements that are .mif files. Alternatively it could be possible to make Vivado avoid including .mif files in the compile order output but I do not know how to do that so you would have to investigate. My feeling is that just ignoring the files in Python by modifying the vivado_util.py is probably easier.

I now have some questions before moving forward. In most cases we *want* requests to handle gzip decompression for us.  I think anyway.  I've seen a few cases of http server configurations gzipping `.tar.bz2` files, or applying other compression methods, before sending them over the wire.  This is also dumb server configuration of course.  But in those cases we want requests to pull that extra layer of compression off for us.

If my understanding is correct, there are two mutually exclusive options here.  We can try to guess what the intent of the server was, even if it's giving bad headers, or we can assume that the headers accurately represent the content, even if they somehow represent a "dumb" configuration.

Thanks for the ping. It's true that this is a Bioconda-specific thing. So, the gist is that for historical reasons there are outdated R packages in `bioconda` with newer versions/builds of those being available in `conda-forge` and by giving `bioconda` the highest channel priority, we are forcing `conda` to strongly prefer those outdated builds. In your case those outdated R 3.3.2 packages probably depend on `readline 6.*` but didn't specify that constraint and thus don't work with `readline 7.0` being installed.
So, there are some pros and cons to putting `bioconda` before `conda-forge` or the other way around. The current state of the Bioconda docs still says to give `bioconda` the higher priority. In reality (due to the issue you just encountered) in often makes sense to use
```
channels:
  - conda-forge
  - bioconda
  - defaults
```
though. (`defaults` has to be in the mix because `conda-forge` is not yet self-contained and depends on packages only available in `defaults`.)

1. We have some documentation of these modules [here](https://yahoo.github.io/TensorFlowOnSpark/).  That said, at the highest level, `TFCluster` is the API that you will use from the Spark driver side to launch the cluster.  `TFManager` is mostly an implementation detail that you shouldn't need to code against.  Similarly `TFSparkNode` mostly represents the TF node from the "Spark side" (e.g. feeding data to the TF process), and you shouldn't code against.  `TFNode` was originally intended to provide "TensorFlow side" APIs for interacting with the "Spark side", e.g. `TFNode.DataFeed`.  That said, the `TFNode` APIs have now been bundled into the `TFNodeContext`, which is related to your question (3).

2. Yes, `main_fun` and `map_fun` are the same thing.  Essentially, we need to convert the standard `main` function in TF code to a signature that accepts the args and ctx.

3. In the past, the `ctx` arg contained metadata about that specific node's role in the cluster, e.g. ip_address, port, job_name, task_index, etc.  Now, it also bundles the older `TFNode` APIs, s.t. you can access them directly from the `ctx` object, e.g. `ctx.get_data_feed()` vs `TFNode.DataFeed(ctx.mgr)`.

4. The MNIST example actually does this, but the Spark driver side was pulled out into a separate `mnist_spark.py` file, whereas the TensorFlow main was left in an `mnist_dist.py` file.  For a "single-file" example, you can look at the [CIFAR-10 example](https://github.com/yahoo/TensorFlowOnSpark/blob/master/examples/cifar10/cifar10_multi_gpu_train.py)

5. You should be able to request to join the [Google group](https://groups.google.com/forum/#!forum/TensorFlowOnSpark-users).  If not, I can try to send you an invite explicitly (although that's never been needed).

I would like to start a discussion on the ExperimentContainer and nesting of Datasets. 

Besides previous discussions the following comments are related to this idea. 
@alan-geller 
>  Perhaps each DataSet should have a GUID that uniquely identifies it?

@nataliejpg 
> something with DataSet ids and having the final 'experiment' put the ids of the calibration datasets in the metadata of the 'final experiment dataset'? Might be cleaner than having nested DataSets. Also perhaps this is can be somewhat abstracted to the ExperimentContainer which I know nothing about but could in my imagination know about these things and be able to do things like 'update_calibration_dataset_ids()'.

I think there is a general consensus that it should be possible to group DataSets. @giulioungaretti proposes an Experiment Container which is a "file system on steroids".  The current discussions raise that we want to be able to group some closely related experiments. 

I would propose that instead of creating 3 or more different layers for experiments (Container, to be discussed and DataSet), we instead make it possible to nest DataSets within Datasets. This could be as simple as allowing the GUID of the DataSet (as a string identifier) to be allowed as an entry in a DataSet. The viewer can then take care of opening the desired other dataset when required. 

The different DataSets can still be different files, allowing easy transfer of only part of the Data and grouping them into one or more Experiment Containers. 

Another advantage of solving this problem by nesting as opposed to adding another layer is that one only has to learn one abstraction and that the superpowers @giulioungaretti is talking about will be applicable to searching the original dataset directly. 

Looking forward to everyone's thoughts

Here is a real world example for an instrument with two parameters volt and curr

The output buffer on the instrument contains a reply to a command that has never been read i.e. "1.0e1V\n". This is not read due to a crash at an earlier run. 
They you ask the instrument `CURR?`
The _recv will then get"1.0e1V\n" or perhaps "1.0e1V\n5.0A\n` following the loop construct.
In the first case we will get the wrong result in any case with both solutions. In the second case you will return `1.0e1V` and keep `5.0A` in the internal buffer and return that on the next command.  
 
It's not about 2 or more chunks that's already handled correctly we are in any case reading until a termination char in this PR. It is only about two or more termination characters within one message. And as far as I can see your solution will still not handle that hypothetical situation correctly. If a command to the instrument returns two partial results separated by a newline char the ask command will still only return the first part and the next one will be left in the internal buffer of the Instrument for the next ask command giving an incorrect result here.

These illegally passed bindings sometimes have to be forgotten, to fix scoping rules. Here we forgot too much. Your solution was very close @joernhees!

Although the examples given here now all work, I am not totally confident I have gotten them all. I think there are probably more edge-cases with sub-queries and/or exists clauses where the internal variables sort of have their own space. (I have more or less ignored the "substitute" function defined here: https://www.w3.org/TR/sparql11-query/#sparqlAlgebraEval) Doing https://github.com/RDFLib/rdflib/issues/619 will probably highlight more.

Finally I got this working. It was actually a matter of applying the correct the Xcode Build settings for the following, after building the static library.

I built the iOS libraries with the following command:
`./build.sh --stdlib=libc++ --archs="armv7 arm64 x86_64"` 
which generates the correct libraries, including the fat lib under `lib`.

The settings that I had to apply for the Xcode project are:

```
Enable BitCode --> NO
Other linker flags --> -lz -lstdc++
Header Search Paths --> /usr/local/include
```

It was the lack of `stdc++`linker flag and  NOT disabling bit code which were causing the undefined undefined symbols for `x86_64`  errors.

![screenshot 2016-10-19 16 47 07](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/4158417/19516852/b50ea7f0-961b-11e6-871f-5d10935aff35.png)

Ok, found it. The problem is that if vmprof samples the stack in the middle of a switch, it simply reads nonsense: most of the time it results in a segfault, but sometimes it might end up in a infinite cycle.

If my theory is correct, it is unrelated to [PyPy issue 2683](https://bitbucket.org/pypy/pypy/issues/2683/infinite-traceback-with-greenlets).

Fixed by this [PyPy commit](https://bitbucket.org/pypy/pypy/commits/927cc69f4d52c669493378b49433b28a4007523c)

I think that in theory this could happen also on CPython, if vmprof triggers in the middle of a greenlet switch. However, I never managed to reproduce the crash, so for now I'm just closing the issue.

import os
import sys
import zmq
import json
import time
import Queue
import logging
import traceback


from cStringIO import StringIO
from zmq.eventloop.ioloop import IOLoop, PeriodicCallback
from zmq.eventloop.zmqstream import ZMQStream
from logging.handlers import WatchedFileHandler, SysLogHandler

formater = logging.Formatter('%(asctime)-15s xxxxx[%(process)d:%(thread)d]:
%(message)s')
syslog = SysLogHandler(address='/dev/log',
facility=SysLogHandler.LOG_LOCAL3)
syslog.setFormatter(formater)
console = logging.StreamHandler(sys.stdout)
console.setFormatter(formater)
log = logging.getLogger(__name__)
log.setLevel(logging.DEBUG)
log.addHandler(syslog)
log.addHandler(console)


data_queue = Queue.Queue(1000)

LOG_DIR = 'test_a'
POST_DIR = 'test_p'
LOG_TYPE_PUB_PATH = 'xxxxx'
LOG_TYPES_PERSISTENT = ['A', 'B', 'C']
ZMQ_RCV_HWM_SIZE = 500

def get_logger(log_dir, log_type='access'):
    prefix = os.path.join(log_dir, log_type)
    formater = logging.Formatter("%(message)s")
    handler = WatchedFileHandler(prefix)
    handler.setFormatter(formater)
    logger = logging.getLogger('varnishlog-%s' % log_type)
    logger.addHandler(handler)
    logger.setLevel(logging.INFO)
    return logger


LOG_PERSISTENT_STATUS = {
    "recv_blocks": 0,
    "recv_bytes": 0,
    }

post_key = 'C'
loggers = {key: get_logger(LOG_DIR, log_type=key) for key in
LOG_TYPES_PERSISTENT if key != post_key}
if post_key in LOG_TYPES_PERSISTENT:
    loggers[post_key] = get_logger(POST_DIR, log_type=post_key)

io_bufs = {key: StringIO() for key in LOG_TYPES_PERSISTENT}
io_bufs_time = {key: time.time() for key in LOG_TYPES_PERSISTENT}


def recv_msg(msg):
    try:
        try:
            LOG_PERSISTENT_STATUS['recv_blocks'] += 1
        except:
            LOG_PERSISTENT_STATUS['recv_blocks'] = 1

        try:
            LOG_PERSISTENT_STATUS['recv_bytes'] += len(msg[1])
        except:
            LOG_PERSISTENT_STATUS['recv_bytes'] = len(msg[1])

        try:
            LOG_PERSISTENT_STATUS[msg[0]] += len(msg[1])
        except:
            LOG_PERSISTENT_STATUS[msg[0]] = len(msg[1])

        loggers[msg[0]].info(msg[1])
    except:
        log.error("%s" %
''.join(traceback.format_exception(*sys.exc_info())))


if __name__ == '__main__':
    loop_instance = IOLoop.instance()
    ctx = zmq.Context.instance()
    sock = ctx.socket(zmq.SUB)
    sock.set_hwm(ZMQ_RCV_HWM_SIZE)
    sock.connect(LOG_TYPE_PUB_PATH)
    for key in LOG_TYPES_PERSISTENT:
        if isinstance(key, str):
            sock.setsockopt(zmq.SUBSCRIBE, key)
        elif isinstance(key, unicode):
            sock.setsockopt_string(zmq.SUBSCRIBE, key)
        else:
            log.warn("... to set subscribe error:%s" % key)
    sock = ZMQStream(sock, loop_instance)
    sock.on_recv(recv_msg)
    loop_instance.start()

A complete different idea, why not just mock the call to the `algoliasearch` python package ?

Example:

```python
from unittest.mock import patch, call

def test_save_signal(self):
    with patch('algoliasearch.index.Index.save_object') as mocked_save_object:
        Website.objects.create(name='Algolia', url='https://www.algolia.com')
        Website.objects.create(name='Google', url='https://www.google.com')
        Website.objects.create(name='Facebook', url='https://www.facebook.com')

    mocked_save_object.assert_has_calls([
        call({'name': 'Algolia'}),
        ...
    ])
```

This way, we avoid unnecessary calls to the API and avoid waiting for the answers.

While I do think calling the API and check the responses are necessary in some cases (ie the indexes settings parameters checks), I don't think it's necessary in this kind of tests since the `algoliasearch` package already have theses things tested.

Mandatory vs. Optional is determined by how input parameters are added to the Rule.  The "--input-parameters" flag is used to create required parameters (and so it is assumed that all existing rules have required parameters), and a new --optional-parameters flag allows you to specify optional inputs.  Mandatory vs. Optional is enforced on the CFN parameters, and if optional parameters are not specified in the CFN the Rule parameters are not added, as per the service team standards.

@jrstarke  that's right, ``AWS::Serverless::Function`` does create implicit resources including an IAM Role (and Lambda Permissions in case you define an API). Best way to capture these resources as documentation gets updated is to simply [describe resources that Cloudformation created](http://docs.aws.amazon.com/cli/latest/reference/cloudformation/describe-stack-resources.html). 

My suggestion on having an IAM role within the template is that it allows you the flexibility to add Managed Policy (multiples) **and** Inline policies at the same time since it's a cloudformation resource and not a SAM translation, of which also helps in maintainability for those reading and using that SAM template - In fairness, it's a judgement call as both get the job done :)

@ktruckenmiller Following the example above you can add multiple managed policies as follows - Simply include the full ARN whether that be customer or AWS managed policy:

```yaml

    ServiceXLambdaRole:
      Type: "AWS::IAM::Role"
      Properties:
        # Managed Policy include required Lambda Execution Role permissions
        ManagedPolicyArns:
            - "arn:aws:iam::aws:policy/service-role/AWSLambdaBasicExecutionRole"
            - "arn---policy2"
            - "arn---policy3"
        AssumeRolePolicyDocument:
          Version: "2012-10-17"
          Statement:
            -
              Sid: "AllowLambdaServiceToAssumeRole"
              Effect: "Allow"
              Action:
                - "sts:AssumeRole"
              Principal:
                Service:
                  - "lambda.amazonaws.com"
```

We use virtualenv so we can create an environment for installing packages through `pip` that doesn't affect the rest of your system. Alternatively, we could install `python-pip` through your package manager, and then install packages through `pip`, but I think the first approach is very much preferred.

Also, if you're on Jessie, you can use the [package](https://packages.debian.org/jessie-backports/letsencrypt) in `jessie-backports`. For most people, this should be preferred over `letsencrypt-auto`.

In the IPython kernel, `sys.stdout` is normally a redirection object we've created. Before running each cell, we update it to route the output to the right cell. That's this method:

https://github.com/ipython/ipykernel/blob/75fbaa529ff46da0b4e5cfc5ea6a729f55183594/ipykernel/iostream.py#L303-L304

But we only do that if `sys.stdout` is our own OutStream object. So while your redirection is in place, we're not updating our stream object. So if you undo your redirection and write to stdout in the same cell, it's still pointing to the cell where redirection started. Any cells after that should work normally again.

I guess we could keep another reference to the stdout and stderr objects, so we can update them even when they're not set as `sys.stdout` and `sys.stderr`.

[`@ellisonbg` commented](https://github.com/ipython/ipython/issues/805#issuecomment-3388768): On Fri, Jan 6, 2012 at 2:26 AM, Min RK
reply@reply.github.com
wrote:

> It seems to me that explicit nesting is _less_ complex than implicit nesting based on the level and location of header cells.  Isn't it easier to flatten a nested structure than to nest a flat structure?  I imagine we will need both notions internally, so should we not pick the one from which is is most sensible to make the other?

From the computer science/data structure perspective, I completely
agree that the theoretically "correct" data structure for the notebook
is a tree.  But there are important reasons why this is not the right
model for the notebook.
- When it comes to authoring documents of almost any kind (pen/paper,
  Word, Google Docs, Latex) the model that users are used to is a linear
  sequence of text and headings with any hierarchy being only implicitly
  defined by how heading levels are used.  Presenting an actual
  hierarchy to users requires them to track that information in their
  head increasing the cognitive load.  They have to translate thoughts
  like "I want to move section 1.B after section 1.C and rename it
  section 2" to their nested equivalent.
- When I compare my experience in authoring documents in Mathematica
  (explicit nesting) with LyX (flat, implicit nesting) there is a huge
  difference.  In LyX, I just set the heading level of a section and
  move on.  Once those headings are set, I can just type content without
  any thought as to what level of nesting it appears at. With
  Mathematica, representing sections using explicit nesting is horribly
  painful - so much so that I avoid using it if at all possible.  When
  you edit nested documents there are some operations (insertion/paste
  is the worst, but also with indent/dedent) that are ambiguous.
  Mathematica tries to guess how you want to handle these operations,
  but inevitably, you end up fighting with it.  This is not a problem
  with Mathematica per-se, but with the underlying model which itself
  has these ambiguities.
- UI complexity.  If you have a deeply nested, real hierarchy, you
  have to reflect that in the UI.  Mathematica uses a sequence of
  vertical lines on the R side of the document that span the
  section/cells they group together.  For one level of nesting this
  isn't bad.  But for multiple levels of nesting, you end up with a rats
  nest of vertical lines and it become extremely difficult to figure out
  which one you want to click to manage things.
- Implementation complexity.  The linear sequence of cells is simple
  to implement in both the notebook format and the UI.  I am
  particularly concerned about the UI becoming too complex that we can't
  implement it in a clean robust manner in a reasonable amount of time.

> I also think performing actions on a group is more straightforward with nested structures (e.g. increase/decrease level, relocation, collapse/expand) because you invariably have the whole group as a single element in the structure, no matter what level.  When the list is flat, you have to reconstruct the group every time you perform group actions.

While agree at the theoretical level, I have never found this to be
the case in authoring documents.

A RelatedManager (like FailureLine.matches) has a `add` method that you can use to associate new objects. You don't have to specify the attribute corresponding to self because it's implicit and the orm will add it for you. `add` also supports bulk insertions using positional arguments. Basically you can get rid of this method.

Seems like a luigi parameter in one of your tasks is of type list. Luigi is attempting to write a summary of the task and stumbling whilst trying to hash this parameter. You MIGHT be able to work around this issue and keep your list parameter by declaring the parameter as insignificant with [significant=False]. Making a parameter insignificant ignores it when testing task equality and excludes it from certain reporting output. If that doesnt work you may be forced to refactor that list as some kind of alternative structure that is hashable (maybe a comma-separated string?)

Here are two ideas of what one can do that simplifies the code base and is taking us in the right direction.

## 1. Remove `is_tmp=` kwarg to targets.

Currently we have a "convenience" kwarg `is_tmp`. Works like this:

        t = luigi.LocalTarget(is_tmp=True)
        args = ['python', 'test/cmdline_test.py', 'WriteToFile', '--filename', t.path, '--local-scheduler', '--no-lock']
        self._run_cmdline(args)

See how convenient it is that you don't even need to supply a path? :)

Now why I don't like this: In luigi we encourage usage of targets like this:

```
class MyTask(luigi.Task):
    def output(self): return LocalTarget(...)
    def run(self): do_something(self.output().path); 
```

But the `is_tmp` thing is directly breaking the `MyTarget(*args, **kwargs) == MyTarget(*args, **kwargs)` invariant.

## 2. Unify `-luigi-tmp-%09d' % random.randrange(0, 1e10)` implementations:

It's used here:

```
~/vng/repos/luigi master  
❯ ag luigi-tmp
luigi/contrib/hadoop_jar.py
49:                y = luigi.contrib.hdfs.HdfsTarget(x_path_no_slash + '-luigi-tmp-%09d' % random.randrange(0, 1e10))

luigi/contrib/ftp.py
169:            tmp_path = folder + os.sep + 'luigi-tmp-%09d' % random.randrange(0, 1e10)
187:        tmp_local_path = local_path + '-luigi-tmp-%09d' % random.randrange(0, 1e10)
261:            self.__tmp_path = self.path + '-luigi-tmp-%09d' % random.randrange(0, 1e10)

luigi/contrib/ssh.py
257:        tmp_path = path + '-luigi-tmp-%09d' % random.randrange(0, 1e10)
271:        tmp_local_path = local_path + '-luigi-tmp-%09d' % random.randrange(0, 1e10)
288:        self.__tmp_path = self.path + '-luigi-tmp-%09d' % random.randrange(0, 1e10)

luigi/file.py
42:        return path + '-luigi-tmp-%09d' % random.randrange(0, 1e10)
105:            path = os.path.join(tempfile.gettempdir(), 'luigi-tmp-%09d' % random.randint(0, 999999999))
147:        tmp = LocalTarget(new_path + '-luigi-tmp-%09d' % random.randrange(0, 1e10), is_tmp=True)
```

I think it also should use `.format()` like this  `'-luigi-tmp-{:010}'.format(random.randrange(0, 1e10))`

But a more complete implementation already exists for [hdfs](https://github.com/spotify/luigi/blob/88edff158d906e6fdb03429dac0a79e64c55983e/luigi/contrib/hdfs/config.py#L101-L143). In particular it adds an optional `<username>` and can handle input that look like this `hdfs://123.456.0.1/my/path/to/file.txt?that=has,query=args`

> it looks nice and clean but too immature.

I'm not expecting aiohttp to adopt this immediately. What I *am* trying to do tho is to kickstart a productive conversation around having a clean, minimal and well-specified interface between server implementations and application frameworks.

We have that for synchronous frameworks with WSGI, but there's no equivalent for asyncio, and as a result all the asyncio web frameworks that exist are each reimplementing all the gnarly details of server handling from scratch.

A far better situation would be if we had a really nicely specified interface, so that we can properly separate server implementations from application frameworks. The messaging interface described in the uvicorn documentation is an example of what that interface could look like.

> that is just one file web_protocol.py

It's at least `worker.py`, `web_protocol.py`, `_http_parser.pyx`, `_websocket.pyx` and possibly some other bits. Given aiohttp's maturity I'm not expecting that you folks would want to remove the existing built-in server anytime soon, which is why I'm starting off by demonstrating a proof-of-concept that exposing a `(message, channels)` coroutine interface alongside the existing built-in server is something that you'd be able to do.

If it's any help, I worker around this problem by setting `no_delay=False`.

I'm not sure why there is a problem with this, and maybe we need to draw attention to asyncio maintainers, but my spider sense tells me this is somewhat of an anti-pattern with a proactor:

```python
    def _set_nodelay(self, value):
        ...
        transport.pause_reading()
        raw_sock.setsockopt(socket.IPPROTO_TCP, socket.TCP_NODELAY, flag)
        transport.resume_reading()
```

Because of the nature of (true) asynchronous I/O, there's no way you can guarantee that I/O operations will not complete between  the calls to `.pause_reading()` and `.resume_reading()`.

My impression is also that the parameters functionality is provided in cases where the user wants to provide many *literal* values to plug into a single query. I've investigated this further, here are my findings: - `_p_jar.cacheMinimize()` removes all unmodified objects from the pickle cache. This is quite radical and may remove too many objects, which could slow down everything. - `_p_jar.cacheGC()` reduces the pickle cache size to the configured threshold (`zodb-cache-size` and `zodb-cache-size-bytes`). - Reducing the pickle cache size in a long running request works best when combined with a `transaction.savepoint`. #### Interpretation and thoughts - Goal: we want to be able to control the maximum amount of memory used while still doing the job as fast as possible. There are installations with a lot of memory but a lot of data and a short maintenance window, but there are also instances with few memory but more time. - Therefore: we don't want `ftw.upgrade` to strictly control the memory, but we want it to help optimizing it depending on the deployment configuration. - `savepoint` + `cacheGC` works well when the cache sizes are configured correctly. - We should stop using `zodb-cache-size`, as the size distribution is unpredictable, but use `zodb-cache-size-bytes` instead. - `zodb-cache-size-bytes` should be about 1/3 of the target memory usage. #### Change: I propose that we just add a `cacheGC()`-call after creating a savepoint in order to give the pickle cache a chance to optimize mid-term of a long-running request. This is not an invasive change, but it can only work correctly when the cache sizes are configured well. (I will update this PR accordingly). #### Graphs: - All update the security on a bunch of objects. - The objects are in a separate zodb mount point; cache sizes apply to this mount point. - After each 1000 objects a savepoint is created and "cleanup function" is called. | # | Cleanup function | `zodb-cache-size` | `zodb-cache-size-bytes` | |---|------------------|-------------------|-------------------------| | 1 | `cacheMinimize` | `300000` | none | | 2 | `cacheGC` | `300000` | none | | 3 | `cacheGC` | `3000` | none | | 4 | `cacheGC` | `0` | `333333` | **1:** ----- **2:** ----- **3:** ----- **4:**

While it's technically allowed by Cloudformation, I don't think it provides the behavior you're looking for.  Take this CFN template:

https://gist.github.com/phobologic/486929b206180b164358

If you upload that, and provide the `prod` value to the Parameter, you'll see that no Output shows up at all.  If you provide `dev` or `stage` the not in prod output gets created.  The only reason I can think that is, is because the `Outputs` section is a json 'object' (in python, a `dict`), so it's semi-randomly (not sure how it picks, it probably isn't order, but who knows) one of the values for the output.  In this case it's always picking the NotInProd one, which means that when you provide values that match it, the Output exists.  When you provide values that don't (ie `prod`) the output doesn't exist at all.

So, while technically this should be allowed, I think Troposphere is actually doing the right thing by stopping people from doing something that isn't what they believe.  Let me know, if you test with that Cloudformation Template, if you see different results.

The reason for it to not work is pre-commit is _not a shell_.  Should `entry: foo {1,2,3}` work?  Should `repo: ${MY_WORKSPACE}/hooks` work?  No, and no.  Just trying to keep it simple, especially when it works just fine to specify the whole path or use `try-repo`.

I don't think you'd distribute a repository with a repo pointing at a local checkout either (and I'd like to discourage it if possible).  This would require extra set up by the end user which would potentially reflect poorly on the tool itself (it is after all supposed to be the thing that bootstraps all of this stuff for you!).

The fact that pre-commit supports local paths at all is a bit of an accident of implementation -- it's incredibly useful for testing repositories but I really don't think it has a strong use case for being checked in that way and used as a long term workflow.

Actually, I think we should just add the other case to this test -- it _is_ a valid way that a user space git could end up in a merge conflict (if you look, we already have two different formats of git conflict output already).

docker / vagrant would be _super_ overkill for this (and making debugging an absolute nightmare).

> Do you know how MULTIUSER_INSTALLMODE_DEFAULT_CURRENTUSER can be undefined here:

I don't know how I could have been this blind, but you can just add
`!undef MULTIUSER_INSTALLMODE_DEFAULT_CURRENTUSER`
to the `modernui` block which comes after the definition of this constant in the pynsist config file. This restores the old installer behavior.

> Including !insertmacro MULTIUSER_PAGE_INSTALLMODE in the ui_pages block gives the user the option to install for all users

If you add this macro without undefining the constant, the default install method will be the user-specific one (the unwanted behavior). However, if you undefine the constant, the default mode will be set to the global installation (the old behavior). This basically gives the user a choice where to install Streamlink while still having the old behavior restored. This would be a compromise.

Any opinions here?
1. Restore behavior entirely?
2. Set global installation as default method while giving the user a choice?

To be honest, I would be very hesitant to include this change. Your "2 spares" workflow would only differ in the certificate retrieval step: instead of copying from active spare you get the cert from the CA! Why not just copy the cert between machines yourself? NB, you already have to set up those 2 machines differently, as one will have to be run with `--skip-authz` so this means you need to wrap all of this workflow in some automation scripts. It seems pretty ridiculous for such generic client to support a feature that can easily be implemented outside the scope of this software. More features leads to more bugs. At this stage `--skip-authz` is a no-go for me.

On the other hand, the code for `acme` library is not that bad and I would consider merging it even if changes to the core client are not submitted. However, it does break compatibility with existing clients such as https://github.com/kuba/simp_le.

No matter what happens, code needs to be improved as outlined in the comments above.

While saving the changes might be a good solution, we need to be careful here. If the save is temporary, it will be reverted on the next run of the client. If the save is permanent, it will create a new checkpoint causing our current rollback mechanisms not work. These are certainly solvable problems, however.

Another approach is to just check/assert that there are no unsaved changes before calling `match`.

Unfortunately always serving gzip breaks HTTP; you're not supposed to send it gzip unless the client asks. It only works if the browser expects and supports `Content-Encoding: gzip`. `curl` and `wget` both explicitly don't deal with gzip by default.

I set up a demo of this at http://com.somebits.s3gzip.s3-website-us-west-2.amazonaws.com/gzipped.txt. It loads fine in Chrome. It fails with `curl` but works with `curl --compressed`. `wget` doesn't even have a way to decompress, you have to do `wget | gunzip`. That's why I think it's better to explicitly name the file `.csv.gz`; the user will know what to do with it.

Lots more detail about this weirdness in S3 [on my blog](https://nelsonslog.wordpress.com/2015/01/21/s3-vs-gzip-encoding/).

Hi all,

I'm just returning back from a long break and looking at this. I had a bit of trouble following the original ticket (particularly the sentence "It appears that these two things do not, at least not obviously"). However, as far as I can tell, the issue is that the YAML file format for atwf is not flexible enough to define some custom workflows, in particular workflows for which you are combining Firetasks in a way that is not already implemented in a Firework already defined in atomate.

@bocklund - can you confirm that this is indeed the issue?

Assuming that's the case, here are some guidelines.

1. If you want to share explicit workflows, the best way is to use the built-in serialization of FireWorks (Workflow.to_file() or Workflow.to_format()) rather than an atomate format. The downside, other than potential verbosity, is that this is really a workflow for a particular *structure* and not a general workflow template that can be applied to any structure. So, I assume this is not really what you want, but I wanted to mention it anyway.

2. I don't agree with just making Firetasks into Fireworks for the sole purpose of letting the YAML format / atwf be able to use them. The real problem is that the YAML format is not flexible enough to do what @bocklund wants to do. To take the specific case of FitEquationOfState, I see it as an edge case. Currently, that task is only used within one kind of Firework in which it is the only task and I wouldn't be opposed to creating an explicit Firework for this (as @bocklund wants to do) rather than creating it "on-the-fly" for the workflow as is currently done. But, given that this Firework is currently only used in a single workflow, it doesn't seem a particularly reusable and I am also OK with it the way that it's written. Basically, I would encourage turning Firetasks into Fireworks if you are trying to create Fireworks for the purposes of reuse in multiple workflows, or if you feel that something is really a higher-level operation than a Firetask and think it deserves to be a Firework. But if it is just for atwf/YAML, then let's please fix the YAML instead instead of just creating a whole bunch of Fireworks for the purposes of satisfying the YAML format constraint.

3. I am very open to additions / modifications of the YAML format. As far as I remember the only change I made recently was supporting workflow metadata in the format and perhaps some minor code refactoring. I think @shyuep initiated the format as a starting point with the intention that someone could make it better. If you want to make the format more flexible to allow composing new Fireworks with a custom set of Firetasks inside, that is certainly encouraged by me. I am not very involved in this format because I don't use the file-based workflow definitions very often. I mainly find it useful to define a bunch of "related but slightly different" workflows in a succint way - e.g., band structure with Boltztrap and band structure w/o Boltztrap - without having to create a new function in atomate for each slight variation. For more complex workflows, I generally tend to reach for Python anyway. But I'd be happy to see someone take it further.

Speaking to the use case of ignoring `dump_only` fields even when `unknown=INCLUDE`: 

We are starting to use marshmallow for our data marshaling and validation for our web API. We have some schemas with fields that we do not want the user setting (via PUT and POST calls), and thus would like them to be `dump_only`. But we would still like the ability to accept arbitrary fields, such as feature flags, without necessarily having to add those fields to the schema.

Being able to ignore `dump_only` fields would add a layer of protection (albeit not the only layer) that these fields don't leak into the model and possibly get persisted on the underlying resource.

For the compiler flags, use `FLAGS`.  This will override the existing flags that SCons would otherwise try to use for your compiler.  

For the linker flags, use `LINKFLAGS`.  SCons may then add additional flags beyond what you give it to link the various libraries that are needed.  You can also use `LIBS` to add libraries to link with `-llib`.

If you type `scons -h`, it will give you more details about these options and all the other scons options we made available.

If you give it everything that it needs to start with, I *think* it won't add anything additional to LINKFLAGS or LIBS.  But for instance, if you don't give it any boost-python library, it will still run through the normal checks to try to find the right library name on your system.  I'm pretty sure in all cases, we have the first check be to add no additional libraries, so if what you give it works out of the box, it shouldn't add anything else.

simple_salesforce supports providing your own instance url and session id as a method of authenticating.

`client_secret` is not a password and cannot be used by itself to authenticate. As [Salesforce documentation ](https://developer.salesforce.com/docs/atlas.en-us.api_rest.meta/api_rest/intro_understanding_user_agent_oauth_flow.htm)says

> These apps can protect per-user secrets. But, because the apps are widely distributed, the client secret can’t be confidential.

 You either need to set up full web server authentication flow with a redirect url, or if all you need to do is identify that you application is logging into Salesforce, you can use user-password flow with simple_salesforce which doesn't require any user interaction.

Like this:
```
import requests
from simple_salesforce import Salesforce

#should be https://login.salesforce.com/services/oauth2/token if you're not in a sandbox
url = 'https://test.salesforce.com/services/oauth2/token' 

#taken from https://developer.salesforce.com/docs/atlas.en-us.api_rest.meta/api_rest/intro_understanding_username_password_oauth_flow.htm
data = {'client_secret': 'yourclientsecrethere',
'client_id': 'yourcrazylongclientidhere',
'grant_type': 'password',
'username': 'yourusernamehere',
'password': 'yourusernamehere' + 'yourtokenhere'
}

response = requests.post(url,data=data).json()

#feed in your token and instance url here
sf = Salesforce(instance_url=response['instance_url'],session_id=response['access_token'])
```

    query_job = client.run_async_query(str(uuid.uuid4()), query)
    query_job.use_legacy_sql = False

    rows = query_job.result().fetch_data(max_results=10)
    for row in rows:
        print(row)
